sentences.text: I was born and raised in the eastern part of Czechoslovakia.
As a matter of fact, in our region which in the 20th century belonged to five different countries.
At my birth it was Czechoslovakia and of course in '38 it became Hungary.
It was a lovely town and
--I never went back.
It was-- it was Uzhorod, when it was Czechoslovakia, and then became Ungvar, when it was Hungary.
And now it's Uzhhorod.
It belongs to the Ukraine.
I come from a very loving middle-class Jewish family.
My parents were not religious but I remember my only grandfather I knew was Grandfather Rosenfeld died at the age of 96 in an accident.
And he used to go to the temple every morning and every evening.
And I remember when I went to his house that he had a rack with these fancy pipes-- long ones, short ones.
As a child I was always fascinated by that.
And I-- I have fantastic memories about my grandfather.
He was a real character.
He always, at summertime, wore these starched white suits like he just came out of a box.
And when he saw the grandchildren, he reached in his pocket and started to crackle the paper that was in there and we knew that there was candy.
And we used to ask him for the candy.
My father was a businessman and he had a textile business and a tailor shop.
He was a very fine craftsman.
My mother, when she was young before she got married, she was already a business woman.
She and two other sisters went to Vienna and they had a millinery shop.
And then when my mother got married, then she became a housewife.
I have a brother who is four years older than myself.
And I had a sister who was five years younger than myself.
My brother's name is Frank.
My sister's name was Clara and my daughter Clara w is named after her.
I was 14 when Czechoslovakia was split up and we became Hungary.
I was 14 years old.
Yes.
It was a small town of about 32,000 population and maybe a third was Jewish.
Well after the Hungarians took over.
Before that I belonged to an organization called Sokol, which was also an athletic Czech organization.
And of course when that disappeared after Hitler donated actually our region to Hungary, then I joined a Jewish athletic organization called Maccabiah?
Oh, absolutely but we kept hardly anything.
There was no kosher room in my house.
Yes.
Yes.
Well--
Of course.
Of course.
They began pretty early.
And you know, we were sort of slowly, systematically reduced to second class citizens.
But for awhile we were still free.
We were still in our homes.
The family was together.
And then they started to take the men into forced labor camps.
And so my father was taken for awhile.
He was later released and came back home.
And my brother was taken to a forced labor camp as soon as he graduated gymnasium and after that he escaped later from the camp became a partisan.
Yes.
Let me tell you that my brother was actually taken from his classroom, from his graduating classroom, with a few other Jewish boys.
They were taken out of the school and they disappeared.
I remember that my parents were devastated.
The government didn't want to tell us where they were.
And my mother hired an attorney and the attorney told my mother they the two of them will do everything possible to find out what happened to these boys.
They found out that they were taken to another city called Munkacs, or Mukacevo.
And they were taken in one of these-- to one of these castles that they Hungarians used as a torture place.
And they beat up and tortured these boys, accusing them of dispersing communist propaganda.
Now my brother had never any political affiliation with anything.
So we knew that it's just a, you know.
[INAUDIBLE]
He was.
Finally my parents paid and released all the boys.
And then they came back and they graduated gymnasium.
Well, later.
Yes.
Later we had to wear yellow stars and we had a curfew.
My father's business was taken away from him.

I would like to tell you a little incident about my father's business because at one point there was a non-Jewish family, a shoemaker, who had about nine children, came to my father and asked him to take his 13 year old boy as an apprentice in his business.
And my father took pity on him and of course took him in.
Not only took him in as an apprentice, but he brought him in our house and he grew up with us as a brother.
And at the end when we were taken to the brick factory, to the ghetto, my father wrote a letter to him to send us some supplies and the answer was that he's not going to risk his life for us.
Now this is a young man who took over my father's business.
When they said that a Jew can no longer own a business, then my father said, 'Well, this is a Gentile son of mine.
I can trust him.' And he gave him the business and the money.
He owned everything we owned at that point, and he wouldn't help us.
So these are some of the stories about our neighbors and friends and how they supported us when we were in trouble.
Well, yeah.
Yes.

Let me tell you that in 1940, as early as in 1940, those Jews in my hometown and in our region who couldn't prove that their ancestors were there in 17-- I don't remember exactly the date-- those were deported.
They were just put over the Polish border.
And some of them came back and they were telling us about the murders and what's going on in Poland.
So we knew that in Poland they are shooting and burying Jews in mass graves.
But we had absolutely no knowledge about the [INAUDIBLE] conference or about extermination camps being built.
Deportations begin in 1944.
Actually in April.
We were taken to the border of Poland by the Hungarians and there the Germans took over and escorted us into Auschwitz-Birkenau.
By train, yes.
Let me tell you that we were herded into cattle cars in my hometown yet.
My brother was no longer with us.
He was in Hungarian forced labor camp.
But my parents and my sister and I were in a cattle car were there about 120 people.
By the way my grandfather died, I told you, at the age of 96 earlier.
So he was spared fortunately of all this.
So the ride to-- the journey to Auschwitz was horrifying.
The cattle car was packed.
There was hardly any breathing space and hardly any breathing air because there was a bucket which was supposed to be our sanitary facility, our bathroom.
It kept overflowing and I don't have to tell you.
Within a few hours, it just kept overflowing.
The cattle cars were never opened to emptied or to get a lot of fresh air into the car.
And so the stench and the air was unbreathable.
And we could actually hear people suffocating in the cattle car.
Of course there was nothing we could about it.
We had no food or water given to us.
And this journey lasted three days.

I also remember during the night the train used to stop and the Nazi's used to bang on the walls of the cattle car, telling us that if we are still hiding any valuables or any money we should give it up or we'll be shot.
And while they were talking to us we could hear on the outside shooting and screaming and we were convinced that they are searching and killing these people.
I very vividly remember this old woman who was sitting on the edge of the cattle car who ripped open her coat lining and reached in there and removed a little gold locket and started to cry.
Opened this locket and started to cry bitterly.
And I being young and still very optimistic and romantic, I was sort of fantasizing that maybe that's her wedding picture in the locket or maybe her family, her grandchildren, whom she left behind.
We were going somewhere not knowing where for how long.
The rumors were that we're going to Germany to work and if the family is healthy and strong, we'll work.
The family will survive together.
So this woman must have been in her 80's, hardly believed that she will never return.
And I thought to myself, how cruel that they won't let her keep this little memoir.
And then she closed this locket and through the cracks of the cattle car, handed it to the Nazis.
I was just 19.
That's right.
Si, let me tell you.

The cattle cars opened and I believing that we're going to Germany to work was very eager to get a good job so that I could help out my parents and my little sister so that they don't have to work so hard.
I was a strong, healthy athlete so I knew I can work.
And I was going to save them.
When I jumped off that cattle car and I looked around, I knew right away that we're doomed.
All I could see for miles and miles were barbed wires.
And you know, so many feet there was always a tower, a wooden tower.
And when I looked up there I saw this Nazi soldier behind the machine gun, pointing it at us, watching every move we were making.
On the loudspeakers the Nazis were telling us to leave all our belongings behind, they are going to deliver it to us to the camp.
And so I turned back to the cattle car and called my sister Clara, who came forward crying, rubbing her eye from the darkness into the light.
And I grabbed her hand and I said to her, 'You hold on to me, no matter what happens.
You and I must stay together.' And she started to cry and she said, 'But Renee, let's wait for mom and dad.' And I turned back to the cattle car for mom and dad, they were gone.
And it was only then that I realized that thousands of people were pouring out of this train.
The train no end.
I couldn't see the end of it.
And there was tremendous chaos.
Children looking for parents and mothers calling their children's names.
It was impossible to find anybody.
And so I kept reassuring my sister that once we get in we'll be reunited with mother and father, so don't worry about it.
And of course we were caught in this human tide and just being pushed towards the camp.
And then suddenly I found myself standing in front of this Nazi officer, a young man, very handsome with a smiling face.
He had a leather whip in his hand, pointing people to the right and to the left.
And it was then that I became very fearful about our future because they promised that we're going to stay together, the families.
And all of a sudden I realized that they're ripping the families apart.
Then my sister and I was pointed to the right.
By the way, this man who separated us was of course none other than the infamous Dr.
Mengele.
We didn't know.
We didn't know it then.
I'll tell you later how I found out who he was.
I had no idea who he was.
And my sister and I were marching towards the camp, not knowing what's coming.
And suddenly my sister noticed that my mother is going to the other side.
And so she wanted to run away from me and I grabbed her and I said, 'Don't go anywhere.
You're going to get lost.
You're not going to find mother and then you're not going to find me either, so please don't go anywhere.' And we were taken into an underground dressing room.
And here, surrounded by Nazi soldiers and vicious dogs that were growling and barking at us, we were told we should get undressed.
We're going to take a shower.
So we were waiting for the soldiers to leave so we can get undressed.
And of course nobody moved, so one of the soldiers walked over to one of these women and hit her and said, 'Take it off.' And we realized that they are not leaving.

So we started to get undressed, and again we were given orders to be very careful how we fold our belongings, remember exactly where you're leaving your clothing because when you come out of the shower, you will have to hurry up and dress and be assigned to your work.
And still we were naive and stupid enough to believe that.
And I told my sister to make sure that she puts her stuff next to mine so we don't get separated when we-- when we come out of the shower.
Needless to say that the minute we walked into the shower room, the doors were slammed behind us.
Our belongings were picked up.
Later.
We came out naked on the other side of the shower room and stood there wet, naked from mid afternoon until about midnight.
I remember my sister and I hugging each other for a little body warmth.
We were freezing.
And then at midnight they took us into this barrack where they shaved our heads, our bodies.
They sprayed us with DDT, with pesticide.
And then we walked through a door where two prisoners greeted us.
One gave us a recycled dress, which we put on our naked bodies.
And another prison had a bucket of yellow paint and a brush about four inches wide and painted a yellow streak from the top of our shaved head down to our back.
This was the identification of being a Jewish prisoner and that's how we entered the campsite.
On the dress, through the dress.
I remember the dress sticking to my body.
We had no underwear, no stockings, no shoes.
No shoes at that point.
Then when we got into the camp we were introduced to this-- to this overseer who was a kapo.
You know, all the overseers were called kapos in the camp.
And she told us to line up in columns of five.
She was a Jewish Slovakian girl.
Told us to line up, that we will be assigned to our barracks.
And when we lined up for this counting, it was [GERMAN] called [INAUDIBLE] appell, being lined up to be counted.
My sister started to cry and I decided to find out where our parents are and when are we going to be reunited.
My sister was the youngest.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, I don't know whether there was in that group any other child that age.
No, of course not.
Of course not.
There were some mothers and daughters who passed through, but those mothers were like my mother's age, 40, 42.
Not older than that.
And I asked this kapo when are we going to be reunited with our parents, and she pointed to one of the chimneys of the crematoria, which I didn't even see until then.
And it was then that I noticed these four brick chimneys bellowing fire and soot.
And she said, 'Do you see this chimney?' I said, 'Yes.' She says, 'There go your parents and when you'll be reun-- when you go through the chimneys, you'll be reunited.'
And I turned to my fellow prisoners and I said, 'What is she talking about?
What does that mean?' I mean, even in my darkest nightmare I could not have imagined the things that were going on here.
But let me go back for a moment because you asked me about Mengele and when did I find out about Mengele.

When we came-- when we were permitted to walk into the barrack from standing outside after the shower, one of my neighbors a young girl one of my neighbors, tapped me on the shoulders and pointed to this Nazi officer who was the same officer we met at the-- on the railroad station.
And she said to me, 'He's asking for you.' And I looked over there and this officer was waving to me.
Now, you must understand we were totally naked.
I still had my hair, my blonde hair.
And I turned away pretending that I didn't notice, didn't see.
And this friend again said to me, 'You better go over there.'
I will never forget.
You know I come from a family where my father I don't think ever saw me in my slip, not naked.
And here I'm standing, how am I going to go over there?
So I cross my arms.
I didn't know where to hide myself.
And when I went over there, he was with another doctor.
I think his name was Dr.
Klein.
And they took me sort of away from the group and took me to a light, which was in the ceiling, and he took his leather whip and put it under my chin and lifted my head with the whip.
And I heard them talking to each other and then he turned to me and he said, 'Why are you here?' [GERMAN].
You spoke German at the time?
A little bit.
Yes.
What I learned in school.
We were bordering with Czechoslovakia so we had two learn German in school.
And so I told him because I'm a Juden.
And he looked at me and then he asked me about my mother and my father, what religion were they.
And I kept saying, 'Jewish.
Jewish.
Juden.' 'How about your grandparents?' he said, and went through all four grandparents.
And I kept insisting that they all were Jewish.
And he got very angry with me and he looked at Dr.
Klein and he said to him-- I remember-- [GERMAN].
That is impossible.
And at that point, I turned back and I saw my sister being already shaved.
So I was afraid that she was going to pass through and I'm going to lose her so I just turned around and walked away.
And one of these kapos came after me and hit me on my back.
I thought my back is going to break.
And said to me, [POLISH] in Polish, and then in Yiddish she kept saying to the girls that I'm lucky that I wasn't shot.
That anybody that walks away from Mengele like that is usually shot.
So that was my first lucky day, I guess to survival.
Did you finally get a chance to go to sleep?
You must have been up for a couple of days with them.
For a long time we didn't sleep.

Let me also tell you that first night when we got into the barracks and we were put up into these three level bunks-- I was on the top of one of the bunks-- there were 12 of us, six facing one direction, six facing the other direction.
And we tried to rest at least.
We were exhausted.
We were frightened.
We didn't know what the next day will bring.
And one of the Polish girls in the barrack started to sing Yiddish.
Now I didn't understand the language.
I didn't speak Yiddish.
But just from the sound of the song I knew what she was singing.
It was the-- my first touch with my Jewishness.
I just somehow I knew what she was saying.
And she was singing the song 'My Yiddish Mama.' And I remember we all cried through-- through the whole night.
[CRYING] That was my first night.
That was my first night.
Next morning we were-- we woke up to the sound of the kapo banging on the bunks, running up and down yelling, [GERMAN]!
Get up!
Get up!
[GERMAN]!
Everybody out!
Quick!
[GERMAN] was their favorite word.
We ran outdoors and again we had to line up to be counted.
It was dark.
It was in the middle of the night. 3-o'clock-- 2-- 3-o'clock in the morning.
And we stood there until about 10 o'clock in the morning.
Was already-- the sun was coming up.
